data.title: My Big Bet On America's Industrial Renaissance: Dirt Cheap 5%+ Yields Poised To Profit
data.description:
The United States is on the cusp of entering a multi-year industrial capex boom, driven by reshoring, the large-scale build-out of AI data centers, the aggressive acceleration of domestic semiconductor manufacturing, and the large scale of energy infrastructure to facilitate that industrial capex boom. 2026, in particular, is shaping up to be an inflection year, as many of the projects that were committed to and planned last year are now reaching the phase of steel in the ground.

I believe that this presents a very attractive opportunity for income investors to capitalize by investing in essential infrastructure businesses (UTF) that own diversified portfolios of high-quality assets that are currently on sale. In particular, two opportunities stand out as being attractive right now, in ONEOK (OKE) and Crown Castle (CCI). Both of these opportunities offer a 5%+ dividend yield with strong per-share growth outlooks for the foreseeable future, along with trading at discounted current valuations.

Why America’s Industrial Renaissance Is Just Getting Started

Before I get into discussing those two picks in more depth, I'm going to lay out a bit more of the macro backdrop that makes them such attractive plays right now, beyond simply their cheap valuations.

With AI and electrification growing rapidly as dominant macro trends, the demand for natural gas (UNG), NGLs (XLE), power (XLU), and wireless connectivity infrastructure is accelerating. Thus, massive factory, data center, semiconductor, and energy projects are underway, while increasing demand for tower infrastructure is also booming due to the rapid growth in demand for wireless data center technologies that these new assets and the ancillary industries growing around them are consuming.

While the industries that these infrastructure assets service could be big winners, there will likely be some losers, as a lot of this investment is speculative in nature, and the future outcomes are far from determined. However, the infrastructure owners themselves are much surer bets because their assets tend to be contracted to lengthy terms, and if their assets are well positioned and sufficiently diversified, even if some of their tenants end up not being winners, many of them will still be able to honor their contracts, and even if not, the infrastructure owners should be able to recontract their assets to other partners.

Three Critical Tailwinds

In particular, three major trends are set to benefit OKE and CCI. Data center power demand is expected to soar in the coming years, which should benefit OKE's natural gas business, and management has already emphasized that the company is in the process of bidding on dozens of deals, and it expects to win many of them. These should provide attractive returns on relatively low capital investment required and should provide a nice tailwind to OKE's natural gas business. Moreover, the LNG export business is also expected to see significant growth in the coming years, which would benefit OKE as well.

Meanwhile, CCI is expected to grow from rapidly rising mobile data usage growth, which is expected to continue to be in the neighborhood of about 30% annually for the foreseeable future, which should be very beneficial to CCI's business by driving strong organic growth at very high returns on invested capital for that business.

Why ONEOK Looks Mispriced Despite Massive Growth Catalysts

OKE is well positioned to benefit from these tailwinds, not only because it is well diversified across NGLs, natural gas, crude oil (USO), and refined products, but also because it has a very strong balance sheet with an investment-grade credit rating, plenty of liquidity, and significant retained cash flow that it is using to pay down debt aggressively. In fact, it expects to reach its target leverage ratio of between 3.5 times and 3.75 times on a run-rate basis by the end of 2026.

In addition, it has a fully integrated business model that goes from the wellhead to water and also generates extremely stable cash flows, with about 90% of its EBITDA coming from fee-based cash flows, which helps to reduce some of its commodity price sensitivity.

When combining its strong free cash flow generation with it nearing its target leverage ratio, and also the significant synergies it's generating from its recent acquisitions, OKE expects to have increasing optionality to deploy significant capital towards share repurchases in the coming years, as its DCF payout ratio is around 50%, which means it is retaining a lot of cash flow that it can use towards either growth CAPEX, repurchasing shares, or even potentially accelerating dividend growth in the future.

Despite these strengths and its exposure to macro tailwinds, OKE trades at just a 9.15 times EV to EBITDA on a next 12-month basis compared to its five-year average of a 10.7 times EV to EBITDA.

This looks even cheaper when comparing it to midstream (AMLP)(MLPX) peers like Kinder Morgan (KMI) and the Williams Companies (WMB), which trade at materially higher valuation multiples despite historically trading in line with or even at a discount to OKE.

On top of that, OKE's 5.9% dividend yield is complemented by a mid-single-digit growth outlook for the foreseeable future on both a distributable cash flow per share and dividend per share basis that combines with an attractive current yield, relatively low-risk model, and attractive valuation to make it a very compelling risk-adjusted buy right now.

Crown Castle’s Reset Could Unlock Explosive Upside

CCI, meanwhile, owns about 40,000 cell towers in the United States that have long-term contracts and annual revenue escalators on them.

The company has seen its stock price plummet recently due to a combination of a forced dividend cut, declining revenues from cancelled leases that were pre-arranged, and its pivoting away from and agreeing to sell its low-return fiber business to refocus on its higher-returning core tower assets.

The company expects its strong operating leverage, minimal maintenance CAPEX, and focus on the United States, backed by strong industry tailwinds, to drive attractive organic growth for years to come, especially when combining the fact that it has 2.4 tenants per tower with ~3% average annual rent escalators.

Not only that, but its expected fiber sale, which is expected to close in the first half of this year, should generate about $8.5 billion that it will be able to use to deleverage to strengthen its balance sheet, while also potentially buying back a considerable amount of stock, given how discounted the stock price is right now. Those buybacks should be highly accretive.

Additionally, while its AFFO per share has gotten beaten down in recent years due to the aforementioned issues, it now appears to have reached a trough, and once the sale closes, AFFO per share should rebound quite strongly through a combination of share buybacks at a depressed valuation as well as strong organic growth in its tower business.

With continued low CAPEX requirements moving forward, CCI should be in a position to further deleverage over time, buy back stock if it remains cheap, and/or invest in acquisitions in its core tower business that should earn attractive long-term returns, especially as it gets incorporated into CCI's large-scale advantaged platform. Not only that, but analysts expect AFFO per share to grow at a mid-teens CAGR through the end of the decade, which should combine with a current dividend yield of a little over 5% to provide a very powerful total return combination to shareholders that could lead to substantial share price appreciation along with attractive current income.

Risk Factors & Investor Takeaway

While both businesses have seen their stock prices drop sharply recently, as I have laid out in this article, I am bullish on their long-term outlooks due to their strong underlying asset bases, favorable macro tailwinds, and combination of attractive sustainable dividend yields and growth potential.

Of course, neither is risk-free, as OKE is still modestly exposed to commodity price volatility, and it could always underperform in its expected synergy realizations. Meanwhile, CCI could face some headwinds in the event of further tenant consolidation and its need to tackle leverage after it sells its fiber assets before it can truly turn its cash flow generation towards buying back stock, growing its dividend aggressively, and/or acquiring new assets to fuel its growth engine.

Overall, however, I think that with stocks like OKE, CCI, and several other high-quality, high-yielding infrastructure plays in the United States, investors right now have a tremendous opportunity to bet big on America's industrial renaissance. This is one of our main themes right now and is why infrastructure is by far our largest position across our 7% to 8% yielding low-beta and high-performing portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: The Market Hates REITs - But You Can Collect A 5.5% Yield From These 3 While You Wait For The Turnaround
data.description:
Introduction

I've you're reading this, then you're probably saying: We've been hearing for years that REITs were undervalued, and still many have underperformed. While I won't argue, I will say that now may be the best time to buy.

Warren Buffett has stated that it's hard to make money in a stock when everyone loves it. In short, this often leads to overvaluation, which could lead to underperformance.

Since 2022, the REIT (XLRE) sector has underperformed. But in the next year or two, I believe this will change for reasons I lay out in the article. Additionally, income-focused investors should rejoice as these REITs' fundamentals remain strong despite their suppressed share prices.

In this article, I discuss three high quality REITs I believe are attractively valued, and why income-oriented investors should consider them.

REITs Amongst AI Optimism

Artificial intelligence has taken the investing world by storm in recent years. From the introduction of ChatGPT in 2022, AI-related stocks have carried the market to a near 17% return in 2025.

While I expect this to continue, I'm confident that the REIT sector will get justice in due time. In the chart below, you can see that REITs kept up with U.S. large-cap stocks from 1998 to 2022.

Since many have underperformed due to higher-for-longer interest rates and volatility in long-term treasuries. For the near term, I expect this to continue due to geopolitical uncertainty.

Currently, base rates are expected to be between 300 - 325 basis points by the end of 2026. As a result, yields on fixed-rate investments will fall. And once these yields reach around 3%, I believe investors will look to rotate back into the market looking for higher-yielding investments. And REITs, with many being undervalued, should benefit.

So, in the next 12 to 18 months, things look promising for the sector, as I think REITs are poised for solid performances. While uncertainty remains, I believe we will see a bit more clarity and stability regarding the economy.

If so, REITs could provide attractive, risk-adjusted returns. Without further ado, let's get into these three REITs.

#1 VICI Properties - 6.4% Yield

VICI is no stranger, as the stock is highly touted here on Seeking Alpha. While some may say the stock is a chronic underperformer, the REIT has continued to see solid growth and raise its dividend year after year.

Moreover, this is supported by improving fundamentals, not deteriorating ones. Despite negative sentiment driven by declining Las Vegas foot traffic and headwinds from their largest tenant, Caesar's Entertainment (CZR), VICI's performance has been impressive. Moreover, their long leases ensure stable cash flows.

In their latest quarter back in October, VICI managed to grow FFO, AFFO, and revenue year-over-year. FFO rose 4%, while AFFO grew 5.3%. EBITDA and revenue grew from $778 million to $825.6 million, while their top line crossed the $1 billion mark.

This allowed management to raise full-year AFFO guidance, representing growth of 4.6% from the prior year. VICI also strengthened its balance with leverage declining from 5.4x to 5.0x and now sports investment-grade credit ratings from all 3 major agencies.

Liquidity was strong at $3.1 billion, enough to satisfy their upcoming debt of $1.75 billion this year. VICI also continues to diversify outside of the Las Vegas Strip. Recently, they entered into a deal with Golden Entertainment to purchase 7 assets, adding exposure to the local market.

This is a good move in my opinion because it captures growth and diversifies outside of the strip. With inflation still above historical levels and strip prices astronomically high, gamblers have seemed to prefer experiences in other areas vs the strip.

This will add $87 million in initial rent with long lease terms of 30 years. Furthermore, VICI still has the strategic partnership with Cain International and Eldridge Industries, providing $300 million in funding for One Beverly Hills.

The 17.5 acre mixed-use development offers luxury shopping & dining and is expected to come online next year. And I expect this to be accretive for VICI as this is expected to draw in affluent shoppers.

For the near-term I suspect VICI's share price will remain volatile due to uncertainty surrounding CZR and foot traffic. VICI received a downgrade due to CZR's risk, declining traffic, and lower gaming revenue.

While management stated they expect improvements in Q1 of 2026, I suspect international travel will continue to impact Las Vegas until at least the back half of the year. However, investors get paid a 6.4% yield that's well-covered by cash flows while they wait.

VICI does face ongoing risk, notably from Caesar's. There have been rumblings of the casino operator asking for a potential rent reduction. If VICI allows this, I suspect high amounts of volatility for VICI with the share price likely falling to the $25 - $26 range.

But long-term, I remain bullish on VICI as I view this as an unlikely scenario. I think FastGraphs' fair value ratio of 15.68x is reasonable.

I personally believe VICI deserves to trade in a range of 15x -16x. At their current P/AFFO multiple of 11.78x, VICI's upside is attractive with a price target of close to $42 by the end of 2028.

#2 NNN REIT (NNN) - 5.8% Yield

NNN, similar to VICI, has also been plagued by tenant headwinds. While VICI has maintained 100% occupancy, NNN has been impacted by bankruptcies. As a result, their occupancy dipped temporarily to 97.5% in their latest quarter. This is down sizably from 99.3% the year prior.

But management stated they expect a rebound above 98% by the end of the fiscal year. Despite this, the REIT raised their full-year guidance and managed to grow revenue & AFFO year-over-year despite lingering headwinds.

AFFO increased 2.4% to $0.84, while revenue grew 5.3%, likely due to embedded rent escalators. Acquisitions also played a role as the Dividend Aristocrat invested $283 million across 57 assets.

They also raised their acquisition guidance from $600 to $700 million to $850 to $950 million. As a result, I expect NNN to continue seeing solid growth for the foreseeable future.

Because they focus on non-investment rated tenants, they're at higher risk of vacancy should the economy weaken further, or we see a recession. But NNN's long track record of 36 years of dividend increases and solid fundamentals gives me confidence in their ability to navigate successfully.

From a balance sheet standpoint, NNN remains comfortable with liquidity of $1.4 billion and 100% fixed-rate debt. Leverage ticked down from 5.7x to 5.6x, and they have $350 million in debt maturing this year. But again, liquidity was solid with over $1 billion available.

If NNN does see more tenants file for bankruptcy, I see more volatility in the months ahead. But I remain bullish as their share price offers an attractive entry point for long-term investors.

At the current price, NNN's forward multiple sits at 12x. Like VICI, I view NNN as of similar quality, and believe they deserve to trade between 15x - 16x earnings.

Using their fair value ratio, the stock also looks significantly undervalued here. And over the next 3 years, upside is strong with a price target of $57.

Agree Realty (ADC) - 4.37% Yield

Last on the list is monthly-paying stalwart, Agree Realty. ADC is one of my personal favorites. This is due to their monthly dividend and attractive yield above 4%. And also because they sport some of the strongest fundamentals amongst peers.

For these reasons, this is why they deserve to trade at a premium. Currently, ADC's P/AFFO multiple sits at 16.39x. The stock has been trading pretty much sideways after falling from their 52-week high of close to $80 in April.

But for long-term investors, this should be viewed as a buying opportunity, as Agree rarely goes on sale. The share price also briefly dipped below $70 earlier this week. What makes ADC stand out amongst peers is their 68% exposure to investment-rated tenants.

As a result, this ensures their cash flows are stable, and should we see a downturn, they are highly likely to continue collecting rent. And ADC's fundamentals should be evidenced by them receiving an upgrade to an A credit rating in August of last year.

Despite negative sentiment towards the sector, ADC, too, raised its full-year guidance. This shows that despite the market's view on REITs, their fundamentals remain strong as all three raised their guidance in their latest quarters.

During ADC's Q3, they saw revenue jump 18.1% year-over-year. Core FFO & AFFO grew 8.4% and 7.2%, respectively, impressive considering the tough economic backdrop. And their occupancy was a near perfect 99.7%.

So, you can see why ADC's premium valuation compared to peers is justified. Their balance sheet was one of the best in the sector, with no material debt maturing until 2028. This gives them ample flexibility to grow the business by acquisitions.

Liquidity was robust, with approximately $2 billion available. This, and their monthly dividend yield above 4%, makes Agree Realty a standout in the sector.

Using FastGraphs' fair value ratio, ADC still offers upside with a price target of roughly $76 by the end of 2028. As evidenced by their A credit rating, near perfect occupancy, and investment-rated tenants, I believe ADC deserves to trade at a minimum 18x - 20x AFFO. This puts their share price around $82 a share using the midpoint, more than 15% upside from the current price.

Risks & Conclusion

Despite my optimism that REITs are poised for solid performances over the next 12 to 18 months, volatility in long-term treasuries will likely keep share prices suppressed.

Moreover, lower base rates could also have a negative impact on cap rates, decreasing investment spreads until they see stabilization.

However, it's important investors pay attention to those with growing cash flows, high occupancy levels, improving fundamentals, and established track records. This ensures that their yields remain safe, and that their dividends will continue growing for the foreseeable future.

Long-term, income-oriented investors should consider these three REITs if looking for higher yields above the index average with strong price appreciation potential.

data.title: Netflix: What If Netflix Never Buys Warner?
data.description:
With all the buzz around the company, I've found myself researching and learning more about Netflix, Inc. (NFLX:CA) (NFLX) over the past few weeks.

I think everyone looks at Netflix as one of their big investment mistakes of the past. A company that had demonstrated good execution for years and had a product known firsthand by everyone, went on to fall 75% from all-time highs in 2022.

While the fears were well-founded, even Bill Ackman himself sold his position at the time, shortly after buying it. If we look at the situation with hindsight, it was a more comfortable position than that held by other very similar companies, for example, Spotify Technology S.A. (SPOT).

The secret to all of this was good management execution in terms of shared accounts and the introduction of the ad-supported account (more on this later), combined with a sublime user experience in its application and outstanding distribution and marketing. To this, we should add its own content, which, unlike Spotify, whose raw material is music, a commodity, Netflix can differentiate itself from the competition.

Broadly speaking, these were the factors that led to Netflix's turnaround. But today, as of January 2026, the company is immersed in a drop of more than 30%, the result of rumors of a possible acquisition of Warner.

While it seems likely that this will happen, there is also the possibility that Netflix will not succeed and will continue to execute the company's own thesis.

In today's article I will try to imagine a future in which Netflix does not buy Warner and the thesis has to be assessed and evaluated on its own.

Network Economy

As I said in my last analysis of Booking Holdings (BKNG), we’re moving toward a world of vertical search engines, and now that AI is easier to train, the key lies in generating proprietary data and owning the distribution. Both of these depend entirely on the size of your user base, creating a network effect: the more users you have, the more data is generated, leading to better-trained AIs, which in turn attract even more users—spinning the flywheel further.

This will be one of the effects of the network economy we are moving towards, but having massive bases of loyal users also creates new business opportunities.

Once you reach a critical mass of users, companies are able to penetrate other verticals, offering a lot of added value to these users at a fraction of the cost. In the case of Netflix, this is happening with games and live events. The initial user base was generated solely through films and series, but now Netflix can diversify into other entertainment verticals, further increasing the switching costs of its platform and increasing the time a user spends on it, generating data and improving the interface and AI, attracting new users.

This competitive advantage allows scaling margins and gaining scale exponentially.

In the case of Netflix, this has been achieved by accumulating content on the platform. NFLX has been able to grow expenses dedicated to new content at a slower rate than the growth in sales, which has generated that all users and marginal price increases have flowed through the P&L, increasing margins and FCF.

As we can see in the image, content expenses have grown at rates below 10%, while sales have grown at 11.8% and EBIT at 22.8%.

This is something I didn't realize at the time. As the content is stored on the platform, keeping the content spend stable, you're making the snowball bigger and bigger and increasing the value proposition per euro spent by the customer. The best part is that this looks like it could continue for quite some time.

And all of this is just Netflix's traditional business; as advertiser ROICs continue to improve, and now taking advantage of Amazon's DPS, ad revenues should increase a lot over time (margins will resemble those of Meta in this segment), although I don't think they will end up being that high.

And that's because the “Standard with Ads” tier has gone from being a tool to reduce churn to a key growth driver, accounting for the majority of new subscriptions in the available marketplaces. While this is very important in itself, the gains from the ad tier are primarily based on usage: more viewing time, more ads shown.

As people continue to spend time on Netflix, the company will continue to collect a tremendous amount of proprietary data. This proprietary data will improve the AI models for ads, leading to better ROIs for advertisers by being able to segment the target audience more accurately. If you increase ROIs, you will be able to charge higher prices and increase margins on this new service.

Unlike the premium subscription, it seems to me that the trick here will not be to raise the price of the premium subscription, but to show either more ads or ads that fit better with the user and those that you earn more, because the advertiser is willing to pay more to reach that user with a higher likelihood of purchase.

Live Events

Without wanting to get into the video game segment, because, honestly, I think it's a weaker segment, the new live events vertical strikes me as a brilliant idea and a win-win for Netflix, users, and advertisers.

Netflix's entry into live events is not a simple catalog expansion but a direct solution to the biggest structural problem of streaming: the churn associated with one-time consumption.

The traditional model: sign up, use and cancel, destroys LTV and forces constant repurchase. Recurring live events (WWE, NFL, boxing) break that pattern by eliminating the “pause month”: there is no natural point to cancel if you want to continue the narrative. Retaining a user for just a few more months has a disproportionate impact on annual LTV, with virtually zero marginal cost once the content is part of the ecosystem.

At the same time, live events reduce cancellations, because the user has a constant reason to stay subscribed. Fewer cancellations and more advertising revenue make the business more stable and profitable. This is why the market is starting to assume that Netflix can make more money with the same number of users, driving a structural improvement in its margins in the coming years.

In addition, live turns Netflix into a utility, not a discretionary library, capturing the last great bastion of linear TV: the simultaneous audience. Events like Jake Paul vs. Anthony Joshua prove its ability to concentrate tens of millions of viewers in real time, which is key for advertisers.

In truth, everything I've discussed so far still applies in the scenario where Netflix ends up buying Warner, but the next part, the valuation part, no longer does. We would be valuing Netflix's core business, and we would have to take into account a couple of considerations that will not occur, in the event that they end up acquiring Warner

Valuation

Based on the nearly $40B in revenues generated by Netflix in 2024, I firmly believe they will be able to double sales by 2030, which would equate to a 12% top line growth rate. With all the optionalities and new verticals we have discussed, I think this is more than likely.

Assuming, as the company said, that ad revenue will be $9B (11% of sales) in 2030, Netflix only needs to grow the number of subs. at 7% and price at 3.1%. Considering that subscribers have been growing quarterly at over 15%, these seem quite affordable figures.

During these years, it is reasonable to believe that EBIT will continue to grow at a faster rate than sales. We have seen the dynamics of fixed costs and margin escalation in the past. If they were doing just over $10B in EBIT in 2024, tripling that would mean generating $30B in 2030. This would mean a CAGR of 19% and an EBIT margin of 37%. The FCF margin, I have assumed, could be around 24%-25% margin.

In the event that the Warner buyout doesn't happen (I talk about this later), I think NFLX could be buying back up to 3% of shares on an annual basis.

If we divide the $30B EBIT by the shares outstanding in that year and apply a multiple (25X EBIT in this case), we arrive at a $206 per share price for Netflix in 2030. I have also added to the EV the $2.8B it would take from Warner, should the acquisition not happen, and the adjustment of the $7.7B of debt.

With all this, the current IRR that we would get would be 17% in 5 years. But keep in mind that this was the scenario that Netflix was considering before the possible acquisition of Warner.

For all these reasons, I rate the stock as a buy.

In the event of such an acquisition, things would change a lot. Figures would swell, growth could certainly be faster, monetization would improve, but debt would explode, and FCF would go to repay it and not to buy back shares.

Risks

The risks of this scenario would be diverse, but I consider that the greatest risk is the competition for free time. Netflix competes against any form of entertainment. Humans have a limited number of free hours per day, but practically an infinite number of options with which to spend our free time. Netflix competes against them all, from reading a book, to meeting up with friends, to playing video games.

Netflix will have to fight to remain relevant, not just in the streaming world against its direct competition, but in the minds of consumers. It will have to continue to offer a value to the user that is significantly higher than the cost of each dollar spent, which will inevitably influence not only its pricing power and ability to attract new users, but also the amount of advertising revenue it will be able to generate by attracting advertisers willing to sponsor themselves on the platform. They need this double network effect that they have achieved so far, but in a world of fast dopamine, it is perhaps increasingly difficult to achieve.

Conclusions

I again remind you that everything written in this article is based on the assumption that Netflix does not acquire Warner, although most of the content in this article would still be valid in that scenario. It could change the rate of buybacks and certainly the user base and user growth, but overall, I think these are positive optionalities. I am one of those who think that a potential merger would indeed generate long-term value for Netflix, but it is impossible for me to make reliable valuation assumptions with the current limited information.

data.title: L'Oreal: Valuation Is Not Cheap Enough To Justify A Buy
data.description:
Summary

Following my coverage on L'Oréal (LRLCF) in Jul’25, in which I recommended a hold rating because I was still worried about the strength of the recovery given the intensified headwinds to some products, this post is to provide an update on my thoughts on the business and stock. In my view, execution has clearly improved, particularly in North America and China. That said, leading indicators are flashing red, especially in high-margin fragrance. The story is improving, but at this valuation and expected growth, I don’t feel comfortable giving a buy rating yet.

Improvements in North America and China

LRLCF performance in the US has returned to solid growth (Q3 2025 saw ~4% y/y revenue growth), and this is more bullish than it seems because the “sell-in versus sell-out” mismatch has finally normalized. Simply put, the phase of inventory destocking is finally over; growth has the potential to continue accelerating based on the demand strength we are seeing, which we can verify is strong by looking at how the Thanksgiving-to-Cyber Monday period performed (e-commerce saw double-digit y/y growth). Aside from that, other data points include LRLCF winning market share from competitors in key categories like haircare, where LRLCF grew at 3x the market rate (due to demand for Kérastase and innovation like L'Oréal Paris Glycolic Gloss). The same thing can be seen in the mass market segment, which was a laggard previously (the Consumer Products Division (CPD)); there was a sharp turn in momentum, now outpacing the market on a year-to-date basis. (data from CMD)

Clearly, we are seeing a resurgence in demand, and the impact on earnings could be a lot larger than expected. As demand normalizes and inventory turnover improves, fixed costs should be easily covered, and that sets the stage for EBIT margin expansion.

For China, I believe we are seeing very positive developments as well. In a market growing only about 3% y/y (per the capital market day transcript), LRLCF still managed to place three of its brands (L’Oréal Paris, Lancôme, and SkinCeuticals) into the top five in Q3 2025. In my view, this confirms that the Chinese beauty market is not simply shrinking; it is simply getting more sophisticated in its purchases (i.e., consumers have become more selective), which I think is largely due to disposable income tightening (poor macro backdrop). In this environment, my belief is consumers will stop trying out unproven brands and fall back to brands that they know will work well for them. I believe this is where LRLCF wins, as they have a strong brand reputation built over the years and are spending (arguably) the most in R&D in the industry (LRLCF spent more than EUR1 billion vs. Estee Lauder, which spent only ~EUR300 million). The results are quality products and brands that offer consumers a “perceived guarantee” of results that sub-scale competitors cannot match.

Tariff

I previously noted the 15% tariff on European imports as a meaningful overhang to the equity story, particularly for the Luxe division. That risk is still real. But what has changed is my confidence in management’s ability to work around this. Instead of just relying on price increases, management is pushing for “tech efficiency,” which is expected to drive a 20–30 bps annual EBIT margin improvement. The idea is to leverage proprietary data tools like BetIQ, which they claim can lift advertising ROI by 10% to 15%.

To be clear, BetIQ is a tool that helps aggregate real-time performance data across thousands of campaigns globally. Unlike traditional marketing tools that review performance after the entire thing is over (often weeks later), BetIQ uses AI to measure the return of every media dollar as it is being spent, in real-time. What this means is that LRLCF (or the brand managers) can immediately step in to cut funding to underperforming channels instantly and reallocate it to high-conversion campaigns. Through this, resources and capital go to the best ROI marketing campaign, and that savings should create a structural margin uplift that helps offset tariff headwinds.

Valuation Disconnect and Deteriorating Leading Indicators

Despite the very positive developments above, I remain neutral on the stock because the alternative data signals are flashing red. Firstly, Bloomberg’s alternative data points to observed sales and web traffic visits down significantly going into the last quarter of 2025.

Secondly, based on Deutsche Bank’s report, supplier data from fragrance players such as Givaudan is in contraction mode. On the former, that points to overall sales weakness, and for the latter, it is worrisome because it suggests the highest-margin vertical within the Luxe division, the Fragrance vertical that includes brands like Yves Saint Laurent and Prada, is seeing weakness. The problem with this is that fragrance is a very high-margin business. A slowdown here doesn’t just drag down revenue; it also compresses gross margin, which may be more than enough to offset the “tech efficacy” point I made above. If you refer to figure 11 above, you can see the LRLCF share price correlates very well with the Fragrance Supplier Manufacturing Index (y/y movements), which supports my point. Also, it doesn’t help to also see slowing Google search trends for “Perfume” and “Parfum.”

As for China, yes, LRLCF is outperforming relatively, but the reality readers need to be aware of is that absolute growth strength is not great. Also, according to Deutsche Bank’s research, the China Credit Impulse (which is a historically reliable leading indicator for retail demand) has turned weak, and November retail sales grew just 1.3% y/y. I do think LRLCF can continue to win relatively, but I find it hard to say with confidence that overall growth is entering a stabilization/growth phase.

Now, all of the above discussion brings me back to valuation. Every story can be invested in at the right price, even if the narrative is poor, but this is not the case for LRLCF. At ~26.4x forward earnings, it feels expensive vs. the expected growth rate (mid-single-digit revenue growth). For comparison, at 26x forward earnings, you can invest in Google or Microsoft (trading at 27x for both), which have much better growth and arguably better business moats.

In other words, I am seeing no margin of safety. Until growth visibly re-accelerates or the multiple comes down to a much cheaper level, the risk-reward simply doesn’t work for me.

Conclusion

In conclusion, my rating for LRLCF is still a hold. It is showing good execution on certain fronts, but the stock is not cheap. I do believe it can continue to win market share and defend margins through R&D strength and tech efficiency, even in a tougher macro. However, the weak alternative data, softening fragrance indicators, and a weak China credit cycle make it hard to underwrite a near-term growth re-acceleration. At ~26x forward earnings, there is no margin of safety.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Garrett Motion Stock: My Best Long Idea For 2026
data.description:
My top long idea for 2026 is Garrett Motion (GTX). The company has a strong multiple-year above-average earnings growth outlook. The stock is undervalued, leaving plenty of room to the upside. GTX has been consolidating in a bull flag formation, which could result in the stock breaking out to new all-time highs as the company continues to grow.

Garrett Motion's Company Background

Garrett Motion operates as a $3.4 billion mid-cap Switzerland-based designer and manufacturer of "air and fluid compressors, turbochargers, high-speed electric motors, and related products." The company sells its products to "distributors and original equipment manufacturers [OEMs] in Asia, Europe, the United States, and other global regions."

GTX is considered a "cutting-edge technology leader." This is due to the company's "highly differentiated solutions for energy efficiency and emissions reduction."

The end uses for Garrett Motion's products are "light vehicles, commercial vehicles, and industrial applications." GTX products are used in internal combustion engines [ICE] that use diesel, gasoline, hydrogen, and natural gas. They are also used in hydrogen fuel cell systems, which have zero emissions. Garrett also has a new market opportunity for electric vehicles and data centers. GTX produces high-power e-cooling compressors for EV thermal management. These compressors are used for cooling EV batteries and vehicles. The e-cooling compressors are also used in data centers.

The next table shows the breakdown of sales by product line:

Growth Catalysts

Garrett Motion has multiple tailwinds to act as long-term growth catalysts. The company is poised to benefit from macro growth. The global gasoline turbocharger market is expected to grow at 8.5% per year to reach about $22.6 billion by 2034. This is being driven by strict emissions regulations. This is causing automakers to produce smaller engines for fuel efficiency and add turbochargers to boost power.

Although EVs have been gaining traction with long-term growth demand, "50% of global car buyers intend to purchase an ICE vehicle over the next two years." Turbochargers are likely to be in demand as emissions regulations get tighter on a global basis. Of course, the EV market could experience a dip in demand in 2026 in the U.S. as the federal tax credits expired on September 30, 2025.

Here's what I think may happen with the auto market in 2026. Many of those who would have bought an EV with tax credits in place, will buy a hybrid vehicle instead to get the benefits of higher gas mileage. Data from Q2 2025 shows that 80% of EV households also own gasoline-powered vehicles, with 15% owning a hybrid. Others who may not buy a hybrid, will probably buy a fuel efficient ICE vehicle, many of which have turbochargers. It is estimated that "50% of vehicles sold might have turbochargers." That percentage is expected to continue to grow.

The percentage of new retail auto registrations that were hybrids increased from 15.8% to 18.9% from Q2 2024 to Q2 2025. This occurred when EV new retail registrations declined from 10.5% to 9.2% over the same time. In Q3 2025, new retail registrations for hybrids surge 20% yoy.

Garrett's electric compressors are likely to see increased demand as hybrid vehicles act as the bridge to full EVs over the next several years. The global electric compressor market is expected to grow at 11.5% annually to reach $11.2 billion by 2034. GTX's e-compressors are used in hybrids to replace the traditional belted compressors for vehicle heating and cooling systems.

GTX also has a growth opportunity with its high-speed electric motors. These are capable of rotation speeds that exceed 200,000 RPM. These e-motors are used in EVs, hybrids, and fuel cell systems in passenger and commercial vehicles. The global market for high-speed electric motors is projected to grow at 3.5% annually to reach $16.6 billion by 2030.

Valuation

One of the attractive aspects of GTX is the bargain valuation level. GTX is trading at only 10.4x expected EPS of $1.75 for 2026. The expected EPS of $1.75 would be about 18% growth over 2025's expected EPS of $1.49. This is significantly below the sector median forward P/E of 17.98x. The stock trades with a low PEG ratio of 0.50. This is also below the sector median PEG of 1.8. I typically like to see PEG ratios below 2 since the growth stocks that I cover tend to perform well at this level. When I see a PEG below one, that really shows a bargain. GTX's PEG is based on the estimated 3- to 5-year annual EPS growth of 23%.

Competitors BorgWarner (BWA) and Cummins (CMI) are trading with higher PEG ratios of 0.99 and 2, respectively. So, I see GTX as a good combination of having strong growth with a low valuation. BorgWarner has an attractive valuation, but its expected earnings growth is only 8% for 2026. Cummins has strong expected EPS growth of 15% for 2026, but the stock is trading at a much higher valuation. Garrett Motion is more likely to have better stock price appreciation due to its lower valuation and higher expected earnings growth, in my view.

GTX's expected EPS growth of 18% can be achieved in 2026 from the expected 3% revenue growth plus from the margin gains that the company has been achieving. The operating margin increased from 13.8% in Q3 2024 to 14.4% in Q3 2025. The net income margin increased from 6.3% to 8.5% over the same time.

Here's a snapshot of Garrett Motion's valuation metrics. GTX is trading below the sector median on almost every metric.

Garrett Motion's stock looks poised to outperform with its below-average valuation and above-average earnings growth. The company's expected 3- to 5-year annual earnings growth of 23% for 2026 is much higher than the S&P 500's (SPY) expected 3- to 5-year annual EPS growth of 11.8%.

Technical Perspective

GTX's weekly chart above shows a breakout from a bull flag formation where the stock made a sharp move higher in October (forming the pole), which was followed by the recent consolidation period since October (the flag). The consolidation period broke higher this week. The breakout was likely the result of Garrett Motion's positive growth outlook and the low valuation level. Bull flag formations like this do tend to result in a continuation to the upside from what I have observed.

The RSI did increase into overbought territory above the 70 level. However, the stock is no longer at the extreme overbought level near 80 where it was back in November. So, the stock has room to run higher from here.

The MACD indicator remains bullish as the blue MACD line remains above the red signal line while the histogram bars remain green. The histogram bars have decreased in length, but this just reflects the consolidation period of the bull flag formation.

The Risks

Garrett Motion has some weaknesses on its balance sheet. GTX only has $230 million in total cash as compared to $1.5 billion in total debt, leaving the company with $1.3 billion in net debt. Garrett also has 1.3x more total liabilities than total assets, leaving the company with ($813 million) in negative total equity. Garrett has run with negative equity for many years.

Running with a high amount of net debt and negative equity puts the company at risk of insolvency if Garrett runs into an issue of not consistently generating positive cash flow. On the bright side, GTX had positive operating cash flow of $445 million over the trailing 12 months. So, the company is bringing in enough cash flow to remain solvent. I expect GTX to continue to generate positive cash flow as the company's growth outlook is positive.

Competition is another risk for Garrett. GTX competes globally with other turbocharger manufacturers and for zero-emission solutions. Garrett competes with large international companies and smaller regional firms. Increased competition can cut into Garrett's market share and make conditions more difficult for the company to grow.

Garrett's sales are concentrated among a few large customers. GTX's top ten customers comprised about 62% of total net sales in 2024. Garrett's largest customer comprised 12% of total net sales. The loss of business from one or more of these customers could have a significant negative impact on Garrett's sales and earnings.

Garrett Motion's Long-Term Outlook

Garrett Motion looks positive as an investment in 2026. The company is poised to benefit from strong sales for its turbochargers, compressors, and related products. These products are all expected to experience growth over the next several years. The loss of federal EV credits in the U.S. can act as a strong tailwind in 2026 for ICE vehicles with turbochargers and hybrids with e-compressors.

GTX's strong above-average expected earnings growth of 18% for 2026 should drive the stock for above-average gains. The low valuation level should help provide the stock with extra upside runway. I estimate that the stock has the likely potential to increase at a rate that is higher than the 18% expected EPS growth in 2026.

The low valuation should help drive the stock for a gain of 25% to 30% for a price target of $23 to $24 in 2026. This would take the PE up to 13 to 14 based on the expected EPS of $1.75 for 2026. That still remains below the sector median forward P/E of about 18.

data.title: Credo's Dip Is The Ultimate Gift For 2026
data.description:
CRDO's Correction Is A Gift

Credo Technology Group Holding Ltd. (CRDO) closed the calendar year 2025 with a 110% gain, having been up over 200% at one point in early December 2025, but it then gave back a large portion of its market cap before year-end. I've covered CRDO a few times, and my most recent article on it was a preview research that called for buying the dip in the stock right before the firm's Q2 2026 report release. After my bullish upgrade, CRDO added 50%+ in market cap, but again, the correction of the last weeks of 2025 eroded all those gains, so now I'm flat on my call:

The dip came with no reason (at least I don't see any), and the correction was likely only multiple-compression-driven. There was no business momentum erosion, so profit-takers drove down the stock's price, making CRDO look like the ultimate gift for buy-the-dip investors in 2026.

A Bunch Of Bullish Catalysts In 2026-2027

What had been driving the AI hype in 2023-2025 was all about hyperscalers' troubles of getting enough GPUs to build out the needed clusters. In other words, the supply-chain-driven issues were forming bullish theses on multiple tech stocks like Nvidia (NVDA), AMD (AMD), Broadcom (AVGO), TSMC (TSM), to name a few. But now, after thousands of clusters have already been deployed, network and connectivity within the built-out capacity are becoming key. If a single link in the system's chain breaks, the entire training run can stall - so a relatively cheap $500-600 cable becomes essential when a training run can cost tens of millions of dollars in electricity and compute time. This is where Credo is actively building its moat.

Historically, CRDO was dominating the copper (AEC) market up to 7 meters, and so with clusters getting physically larger, there was a risk that they'd eventually switch to optical cables, an area where Credo had little presence and expertise. But now, we're seeing Credo enter the optical cables market (through its ZeroFlap optics) by applying its AEC philosophy to optics - i.e., by controlling the DSP (digital signal processor) and the software stack, and thus effectively adding a "check-engine light" for data centers. I'm not an engineer, but that's how I understand it from the comments at the recent Barclays conference, where the CEO shared the real-life cases of the firm's clients.

So ZF optics, ZeroFlap optics, so the ZeroFlap theme is ultimately speaking, to reliability. So we had Oracle approaches about 20 months ago. And they were struggling also with link flap, same as xAI. We say, use AECs, and they saw our links are too long, far longer than 7 meters. And so we're going to have to deal with laser-based optics by definition. And so he said, we'd love to work with you on a solution where we go up the stack together. And the idea is, could you design a system solution where you could provide visibility and telemetry on every single link in the cluster to the point where you could determine the link health on all of the links, so you've got 1 million GPU cluster, could you have a system where all of that telemetry data is being fed real time into the networks of management software so that you could set a threshold and when you saw a link that started declining from a signal integrity standpoint, could you set a threshold where they cross that threshold, could you actively take that link down? Could you take that GPU out of the cluster to avoid the link flap?

Source: Barclays conference

So, in simple terms, CRDO's tech allows network operators to see if a link is degrading before it fails, so that they can jump right to the troubled GPU (without crashing the job of the cluster) and fix it before the issue takes place. Financially, it's a bit early to project the real revenue addition in Credo's books, but I think somewhere in FY2027 (in 2-3 quarters from now), the firm will get a massive new TAM addition, at the very same time keeping the gross margins at the high end of their 63-65% target range (because it's a higher-dollar market for them). This is going to be their extended catalyst throughout 2026.

One assumption of mine that has actually been proving right so far is Credo's deconcentration of sales. In other words, Credo now has 4 customers that each contribute more than 10% of revenue. The largest customer's share is still quite high (~42%), but the trend itself looks promising. I expect it to continue over the next few quarters as the company keeps ramping up its operations and signing new customers. The management mentioned the 5th hyperscaler, which should add a lot in calendar year 2026, so I expect Credo to have 5 distinct 10%+ customers. It's a relief for any possible revenue diversification-related risks that might have been pressing the forward multiples, in my view.

Another catalyst in 2026 that might materialize and settle in the company's financials is the transition to the PCIe Gen6 ecosystem.

This new protocol doubles the data rate again, making signal integrity exponentially harder. And Credo has a product that it can benefit from - at least the management said on the call that they were on track for PCIe design wins in FY2026:

We remain on track for PCIe design wins in fiscal '26, followed by meaningful production revenue in fiscal '27. Our existing AEC and IC businesses both address multibillion-dollar market opportunities with excellent visibility for continued growth. But the truly exciting part of this quarter is that we've added 3 entirely new growth pillars, each representing distinct multibillion-dollar market opportunities that significantly expand our total addressable market and extend the reach and depth of our connectivity leadership.

They're effectively going to compete with Aster Lab (ALAB) and Marvell (MRVL), both of which enjoy high GP margins on a TTM basis (75.41% and 50.71%), so Credo might see its consolidated GP going higher from here, thus continuing the positive effect of the past quarters' operating leverage.

When looking beyond fiscal 2027, I see CRDO benefiting from its Weaver gearbox, which is designed to allow GPUs/XPUs to connect to standard DDR memory over longer distances (up to 10 inches) without losing performance. We could have seen what the soaring prices in the HBM market did to Micron (MU), so I think a similar buying pressure might come for Credo as well when its Weaver gearbox enters the market.

So, as you can see from the above discussion, Credo's got a bunch of positive catalysts that can easily lift the stock to fresh ATHs if at least part of them materialize in time.

Credo's Valuation Update

On a TTM basis, CRDO is valued at almost 124x P/E ratio, so at first glance, it might look extremely expensive even for a GARP-minded investor. But I think CRDO has actually become quite cheap after its post-December correction because its 1-year forward P/E ratio has dropped to 38x:

And the FY2028 EPS of $4.62 implies a P/E ratio of less than 31x, which is relatively small for the level of growth that the current consensus shows beyond calendar 2027:

Moreover, after CRDO's Q2 earnings release, analysts have only gained conviction in the firm's long-term prospects when we look at the 1-month revision dynamics:

I assume that Credo should trade at a 1.0-1.5x PEG ratio when it closes the fiscal year 2027 (in 5-6 quarters). But given the double-beating track record and new product launches that are hardly reflected in the current estimates (because it's hard to quantify them), I think we can easily add 15% on top of what's currently expected for FY2027 EPS. As a result, I get $4.2895 versus $3.73 you can see in the table above.

The implied FY2027/2026 EPS growth is going to be ~54.29% YoY, so if the PEG ratio stands at the midpoint of the 1.0-1.5x, the price target would arrive at $291.29/share:

54.29*1.25 = 67.9x (price-to-earnings)

67.9x * $4.29 = $291.29/share (+106% upside)

Source: Oakoff's calculations

This implies the finding that CRDO needs to double after its recent dip to get fairly valued under my assumptions.

Risks

Much of Credo's future expansion depends on its ability to fulfill its massive order book, which in turn is tied to TSMC's capacity. So, effectively, any major supply bottleneck will likely lead to stalled revenue growth.

Another risk that I see in my bullish update is that my thesis is largely dependent on the assumption of Credo's continued high-quality execution. I bet big on the new products that should enter the market over the next 12-24 months and enlarge the company's TAM/SAM. If that doesn't happen, or if Credo's launch timing lags behind the timeline, the market might decide to continue the repricing process that has been ongoing since early December 2025.

Also, my valuation assumptions might look too bullish to come true. I assume a forward PEG of 1.25x to be fair, but it's already 25% above Peter Lynch's suggested norm. Credo might fall another 30-40% from today's levels - and even more - if its earnings power weakens at some point.

Your Takeaway

Despite a bunch of risks, I think that Credo has become too tempting to ignore on its dip, as the catalysts planned for 2026 look real and significant. The durability of growth seems to be way better than what the market is pricing in right now, and I foresee CRDO reclaiming its ATHs shortly and then pushing toward my $290 price target this year (or next year).

Good luck with your investments!

Global Credit and Financial Bubble Watch:

January 5 - Bloomberg (Gerson Freitas Jr and Caleb Mutua): "Firms sold $37 billion of investment-grade bonds in the US on the first Monday of 2026, kicking off what's expected to be a record year for issuance… Firms are expected to sell $215 billion of high-grade debt in January alone, a monthly all-time high…"

January 5 - Bloomberg (Finbarr Flynn, Harry Suhartono and Janice Huang): "Global credit markets are sending off one of the clearest signals yet that investors are looking past geopolitics. At least 10 borrowers in Asia… were marketing notes in the US currency Tuesday, the most in a single day since September. That's after Monday emerged as the best day for the market in a year, with global dollar bond sales raising a combined $61 billion."

January 7 - Bloomberg: "China is starting the year with a record clip of government bond issuance, rattling a market where demand is already weak. The nation has announced plans to issue 522 billion yuan ($74.6bn) of bonds in three auctions between January 1 to 15. That would be the highest amount for a half-monthly period…"

Global bond sales are having their busiest ever start to a year as all kinds of borrowers seize on investors' buoyant appetite for debt. Corporations and governments in the US, Europe and Asia had already borrowed roughly $260 billion across currencies by Wednesday."

January 6 - Bloomberg: "Wells Fargo was the top underwriter of US commercial mortgage backed securities in 2025 as the value of deals rose 14%. Issuers sold $118 billion of CMBS vs. $103.6 billion in 2024."

January 6 - Bloomberg: "BofA Securities was the top underwriter of US asset-backed securities in 2025 as the value of deals rose 7.2%. Issuers sold $366.1 billion of ABS vs. $341.5 billion in 2024."

January 8 - Bloomberg (Pablo Mayo Cerqueiro, Abhinav Ramnarayan, and Bailey Lipschultz): "Companies around the world are issuing convertible bonds at the fastest pace in 24 years, as the development of artificial intelligence spurs a hunt for cheaper financing. Alibaba Group… and Super Micro Computer Inc. are among the firms tapping convertible securities to fund AI-related bets, helping drive the amount raised in 2025 to around $166.5 billion… That's the most since 2001 and just shy of a historic record…"

January 3 - Bloomberg (Caleb Mutua and Isabelle Lee): "Artificial intelligence spending and the growth of the private credit market aren't just spurring companies to borrow more, they're also helping to generate fresh records for corporate-bond trading. An average of $50 billion in investment-grade and high-yield bonds changed hands each trading day last year… That marked a record level, up from $46 billion in 2024, the latest in a string of records as the market benefits from longer-term changes like growing electronic trading."

Market Instability Watch:

January 7 - Bloomberg (Masaki Kondo): "Japan's government bond market is set for another tough year as investors contend with the largest net increase in supply in well over a decade. The nation's sovereign debt - the worst performer among the world's biggest markets last year - faces an 8% rise in net supply to about ¥65 trillion ($415bn) in the fiscal year starting in April… 'Supply-demand conditions in Japan's bond market have deteriorated to the point that the government may eventually need to adjust issuance each quarter,' said Akio Kato, senior manager of the strategic research and investment division at Mitsubishi UFJ Asset Management…"

U.S. Credit Trouble Watch:

January 9 - Bloomberg (Jeannine Amodeo): "An anticipated pickup in merger and acquisition-related deals arrived in the US leveraged loan market to open 2026, led by a $5 billion offering from Hologic that's part of a much-larger financing effort… However, at just over $30 billion, this week's overall launches are some 40% below year-earlier levels, continuing a more-moderate pace of activity seen in the final few months of 2025."

Trump Administration Watch:

January 6 - Bloomberg (Hadriana Lowenkron and Kate Sullivan): "President Donald Trump asked Secretary of State Marco Rubio to lead the process to implement economic and political reforms in Venezuela and the US believes it is getting 'full, complete and total' cooperation from the government in Caracas after the capture of Nicolas Maduro, White House senior adviser Stephen Miller said… Trump requested Rubio 'under the president's close guidance and direction, to be the lead on this process,' Miller told reporters… The secretary of State will lead a team that includes officials working on energy, financial, and military policy, he added."

January 5 - Wall Street Journal (Annie Linskey and Meridith McGraw): "After overseeing a brazen operation to oust Venezuela's Nicolás Maduro, President Trump is turning his attention to other countries over which he wants the U.S. to exert more control. In the days since Maduro's capture, Trump has renewed threats against Colombia, criticized Mexico's leadership, predicted Cuba's government would fall and reiterated his desire to take over Greenland. Trump has privately told aides that he is thrilled with the outcome of the Venezuela operation… 'He's more resolved, not less, after this success,' Sen. Lindsey Graham (R., S.C.) said... 'We need to clear up our backyard,' said Graham… 'The theme here is: securing the border is really important. We need to get to the source of the problem'."

January 6 - Financial Times (Edward Luce): "Though a teetotaller, Donald Trump has an 'alcoholic's personality', according to Susie Wiles, his chief of staff. Addictive personalities tend to be compulsive risk-takers. Doing what makes you feel good suspends the pain. In Trump's case, that pain is increasingly domestic and the cure foreign. The more dire his situation at home - his poll numbers are bad and show no sign of improving - the greater the temptation of getting a fix abroad… In explaining why he ordered the abduction of Venezuela's Nicolás Maduro last Saturday, Trump cited a US warrant. He sent the sheriff into the jungle to retrieve a fugitive from American justice. It worked like a charm. As long as the target is outgunned, success is assured. Trump is increasingly confident of his unchecked control over America's war machine. Denmark's anxiety is thus justified. Without prompting, he raised Greenland at his post-Maduro appearance last Sunday."

January 9 - Bloomberg (Josh Wingrove, Scott Carpenter and Katy O'Donnell): "US President Donald Trump is directing Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac to purchase $200 billion in mortgage bonds, a move he cast as his latest effort to bring down housing costs ahead of the November midterm election. January 9 - Associated Press (Samya Kullab and Illia Novikov): "Russia bombarded Ukraine with hundreds of drones and dozens of missiles in a large-scale overnight attack, officials said… For only the second time in the nearly 4-year-old war, it used a powerful, new hypersonic missile that struck western Ukraine in a clear warning to Kyiv's NATO allies. The intense barrage and the launch of the nuclear-capable Oreshnik missile came days after Ukraine and its allies reported major progress toward agreeing on how to defend the country from further Moscow aggression if a U.S.-led peace deal is struck."

AI Bubble/Arms Race Watch:

January 4 - Financial Times (Michael Contopoulos): "Meta's 40-year bond sale last year was widely seen as a vote of confidence in Big Tech and artificial intelligence given the strong demand for the debt. Investors lined up to lend for four decades to a company considered among the world's most financially secure. But what looks like confidence may instead be complacency. Corporate bond investors are quietly absorbing enormous speculative risk and that is unlikely to bode well for equity holders. For years, tech's appeal stemmed from asset-light, cash-rich business models. Pristine balance sheets and minimal debt weren't just attractive; they were defining features… But in the scramble for AI capacity, these companies have morphed into capital-intensive utilities with huge infrastructure commitments, both on and off balance sheets."

Bubble and Mania Watch:

January 6 - Associated Press (Levi Sumagaysay Calmatters): "As California becomes more dependent on tax revenue from the tech industry, its stake in the health of the artificial intelligence industry has grown. The state is seeing financial benefits from the AI boom, a new analysis by the Legislative Analyst's Office shows. But the boom raises questions: Will it continue to be accompanied by a decline in tech and other jobs? Is it a bubble? Tax revenue from stock-option withholding paid by some of the state's biggest tech companies made up about 10% of all income tax withholding in 2025, estimated Chas Alamo, the principal fiscal and policy analyst with the LAO."

January 8 - Bloomberg (James Crombie): "Excess liquidity has inflated asset prices across markets beyond just artificial intelligence, according to Richard Bernstein Advisors. 'We're kind of in an everything bubble at the moment,' said Mike Contopoulos, the firm's deputy chief investment officer. 'It's not just AI - it's crypto, it's meme stocks, it's SPACs, it's IG credit, it's high-yield credit,' he said…"

January 7 - Wall Street Journal (Kate Clark): "Fundraising for U.S. venture-capital firms dropped 35% in 2025, the most anemic stretch in at least six years, with money flowing primarily to the most trusted investment firms as companies stay private longer. The $66 billion raised last year represents a 70% drop from the 2022 record, according to new data from research firm PitchBook."

January 8 - Financial Times (Pras Subramanian): "General Motors will take an additional $6 billion charge to its EV business, the automaker said in an SEC filing posted after the bell on Thursday. This comes after softer-than-expected demand for EVs and the loss of the federal EV tax credit at the end of Q3 2025."

Deflating Crypto Bubble Watch:

January 5 - Bloomberg (David Pan): "Bitcoin accumulator Strategy Inc. had a $17.44 billion unrealized loss in the fourth quarter tied to the decline in the value of the Michael Saylor-led company's roughly $63 billion cryptocurrency stockpile."

Inflation Watch:

January 8 - Bloomberg (Matthew Boesler): "US inflation expectations rose in December while perceptions of job availability were the worst in at least 12.5 years, according to a monthly survey from the Federal Reserve Bank of New York. Consumers saw prices rising 3.4% over the next year, up from 3.2% in November, according to a report released Thursday. The probability consumers assigned to finding a job if they lost theirs fell to 43.1% - the lowest in the history of the bank's Survey of Consumer Expectations, which dates to mid-2013."

January 6 - Wall Street Journal (Jared Mitovich): "Nineteen states raised their minimum wage starting this month, providing a pay bump to an estimated 8.3 million workers… Thirty states have now set a higher minimum wage than the federal minimum of $7.25 an hour. On Jan. 1, Washington state adopted the highest state-level rate at $17.13 an hour, becoming the first state to enact an hourly minimum above $17… In Hawaii, baseline hourly rates rose by $2 to $16… In Nebraska and Missouri, voter-passed ballot initiatives brought those states' minimum wages up to $15 an hour... For the first time, more workers now live in states that pay $15 or higher per hour than those that have kept the $7.25 minimum…"

Federal Reserve Watch:

January 8 - Bloomberg (Christopher Anstey and Daniel Flatley): "Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said he anticipates that President Donald Trump decides on a successor to Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell this month. Trump plans to attend the annual forum in Davos, Switzerland - scheduled for Jan. 19-23 - and the timeframe for a Fed decision may be 'right before or right after that - I think in January,' Bessent said…"

January 8 - Financial Times (Jennifer Schonberger): "Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said… the Federal Reserve needs to do its part to boost investment in the economy and that interest rates should be substantially lower. 'The White House can only do so much; at a certain point, the Federal Reserve must also do its part to spur investment,' Bessent said... 'I think that we are still substantially above the neutral rate, and I think that we should not be in restrictionary mode'."

January 8 - Bloomberg (Enda Curran): "Federal Reserve Governor Stephen Miran said he is looking for 150 bps of interest-rate cuts this year to boost the labor market. Describing monetary policy as restrictive, Miran said underlying inflation is likely running at 2.3%, which means Fed officials have room to cut further. 'I'm looking for about a point and a half of cuts. A lot of that is driven by my view of inflation,' Miran said… 'Underlying inflation is running within noise of our target, and that's a good indication of where overall inflation is going to be going in the medium term'."

January 5 - Bloomberg (Catarina Saraiva): "Federal Reserve Bank of Minneapolis President Neel Kashkari said interest rates may be close to a neutral level for the US economy now, leaving it up to incoming data to guide the central bank's actions. 'Over the last couple of years, we kept thinking the economy is going to slow down, and the economy has proven to be far more resilient than I had expected,' Kashkari… said… 'That tells me, well, monetary policy must not be putting that much downward pressure on the economy. My guess is we're pretty close to neutral right now,' he said."

U.S. Economic Bubble Watch:

January 7 - Axios (Neil Irwin and Courtenay Brown): "The final months of 2025 offered plenty of reasons for labor market alarmism. Job creation slowed to a near halt, the unemployment rate edged to its highest level in four years, and the business news wires were chockablock with AI-inflected layoff announcements. But the job market looks more stable than those headlines would suggest. Private-sector data for December points to some stabilization in demand for workers… In effect, the job market showed weakness through the fall before stabilizing and perhaps improving in December…"

January 3 - Associated Press (Christopher Rugaber): "Sluggish December hiring concluded a year of weak employment gains… Employers added just 50,000 jobs last month, nearly unchanged from a downwardly revised figure of 56,000 in November… The unemployment rate slipped to 4.4%, its first decline since June, from 4.5% in November… Nearly all the jobs added in December were in the health care and restaurant and hotel industries. Health care added 38,500 jobs, while restaurants and hotels gained 47,000. Governments - mostly at the state and local level - added 13,000."

January 7 - Yahoo Finance (Emma Ockerman): "US private employers brought on 41,000 new positions in December, according to data from… ADP… Still, December's gains essentially erased November's losses, with revised ADP data from the previous month's report showing private employers dropped 29,000 jobs in November, fewer than the previously reported 32,000… 'Even in those sectors that shed jobs this month, the shedding was not as strong as last month,' ADP chief economist Nela Richardson said... 'In that sense, it's better news than we saw in November'."

January 7 - Reuters (Lucia Mutikani): "U.S. job openings dropped to a 14-month low in November while hiring resumed its sluggish tone… Job openings, a measure of labor demand, dropped 303,000 to 7.146 million by the last day of November, the… Bureau of ‌Labor Statistics said in its Job Openings and Labor Turnover Survey, or JOLTS report. That was the lowest level since September 2024. October's openings were revised down to 7.449 million."

January 7 - Bloomberg (Jarrell Dillard): "US services activity expanded in December at the fastest pace in more than a year, fueled by solid demand growth and a pickup in hiring. The Institute for Supply Management's index of services rose 1.8 points to 54.4, the highest since October 2024… New orders expanded by the most since September 2024 and a measure of business activity, which parallels the ISM's factory output gauge, climbed to a one-year high. Export bookings grew at the fastest pace in more than a year… 'The broad-based strength in the headline index suggests that conditions in the services sector are picking up, hinting at the potential for some more broad-based economic growth,' Alexandra Brown, North America economist at Capital Economics, said…"

January 8 - Financial Times (Zehra Munir): "The US trade deficit narrowed to its lowest level since 2009 in October, as Donald Trump's tariffs continued to prompt fluctuations in imports. The gap between imports and exports of goods fell 39% from the previous month to $29.4bn… That reduced the deficit to its narrowest level in more than 16 years…"

January 7 - Reuters (Neil J Kanatt): "U.S. online holiday spending growth slowed in the 2025 season, Adobe Analytics data showed…, even as shoppers set a record on the back ‌of steep discounts and wider use of buy-now-pay-later plans. Online spending from November 1 ‌through December 31 rose 6.8% to $257.8 billion… That compared to an 8.7% rise in online spending during the same period last year. Adobe said spending beat its prior forecast of $253.4 billion in ⁠online sales for ‌the season."

January 5 - New York Times (Neal E. Boudette): "The auto industry is facing some big challenges... Yet sales of new cars in the United States are expected to have risen modestly in 2025, to about 16.3 million. Several large automakers… told investors… they had closed out the year with strong sales. How is that possible? The auto industry has evaded a slump largely because affluent Americans… continued to buy new cars at a decent clip... Families with a household income of $150,000 a year or more now buy 43% of the new cars sold in the country, up from one-third of all cars sold in 2019 before the Covid-19 pandemic…"

China Watch:

January 6 - New York Times (Li Yuan): "When U.S. forces captured Venezuela's president, Nicolás Maduro, Chinese social media lit up. People with nationalist views asked: Why can't Beijing do the same in Taiwan and arrest its president? On the other side of the political spectrum, people cheered the downfall of a dictator… Within hours, the discourse online became a proxy debate over China's power, its limits and its future. For nationalist Chinese, the U.S. military operation had exposed American lawlessness and frustrations in China at what they believe is Beijing's restraint, particularly on Taiwan… On the social media site Weibo, the hashtag related to Mr. Maduro's seizure rose to the No. 1 position on the platform's hot-search list. It drew over 600 million views in the first 24 hours…"

January 8 - CNBC (Anniek Bao): "China's consumer inflation accelerated in December to the fastest pace in nearly three years as spending picked up ahead of the New Year holiday… Consumer prices rose 0.8% from a year earlier, their highest level since February 2023…"

January 4 - Reuters (Liangping Gao and Ryan Woo): "China's services activity expanded at its slowest pace in six months in December, as growth in new business softened and foreign demand declined… The RatingDog China General Services PMI, compiled by S&P Global, edged down to 52.0 in December from 52.1 the previous month, marking the weakest reading since June."

January 8 - Bloomberg: "China Vanke Co. is preparing a debt restructuring plan at the request of authorities…, pushing one of the country's largest real estate developers closer to default. Vanke… was recently asked by authorities to accelerate its overhaul and submit the plan as soon as possible… Normally a restructuring would lead to suspension of debt payments and result in a default. The developer has faced mounting strains from nearly $50 billion in interest‑bearing liabilities. A restructuring would be one of China's biggest-ever and would be the clearest signal yet that Vanke's largest shareholder, state-backed Shenzhen Metro Group Co., won't be bailing the firm out."

Europe Watch:

January 7 - Financial Times (Olaf Storbeck and Ian Smith): "Eurozone inflation fell to 2% in December, hitting the European Central Bank's target for the first time since the summer and strengthening the case for interest rates to remain on hold… In December, the ECB held its benchmark interest rate at 2% for the fourth meeting in a row."

Japan Watch:

January 4 - Bloomberg (Toru Fujioka): "Bank of Japan Governor Kazuo Ueda used his first public appearance in the new year to underscore his intention to keep raising the benchmark rate in a speech to private bankers. 'We will keep raising rates in line with improvement in the economy and inflation,' Ueda said… 'The appropriate adjustment of monetary easing will lead to the achievement of stable inflation target and longer-term economic growth.' The comments… made it clear that Ueda hasn't finished dialing back monetary easing after bringing the rate to the highest since 1995."

January 6 - Reuters (Satoshi Sugiyama): "Japan's service sector expanded at its slowest pace since May in December… The S&P Global final Japan Services Purchasing Managers' Index (PMI) fell to 51.6 in December from 53.2 in November, worse than a flash reading of 52.5 but remaining above the 50.0 line that separates growth from contraction for the ninth consecutive month."

Social, Political, Environmental and Cybersecurity Instability Watch:

January 7 - Bloomberg (Magdalena Del Valle and John Harney): "President Donald Trump has ordered the US government to pull out of 66 international organizations that the White House said 'no longer serve American interests.' In a presidential memorandum…, Trump directed US agencies and departments to 'cease participating in and funding 35 non-United Nations organizations and 31 UN entities that operate contrary to US national interests, security, economic prosperity, or sovereignty'… The US was withdrawing from efforts to 'promote radical climate policies, global governance, and ideological programs that conflict with US sovereignty and economic strength'."

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
